matches.x: PSALM XXII, David prayeth in great distress, yet he praiseth God, To the chief Musician upon Aijeleth Shahar, A Psalm of David. Y God, why hast. thou forsaken’ me? My God, hear the words of ‘my roaring; thou art far from helping me.

PSALM XXII, David prayeth in great distress, yet he praiseth God, To the chief Musician upon Aijeleth Shahar, A Psalm of David. Y God, why hast. thou forsaken’ me? My God, hear the words of ‘my roaring; thou art far from helping me.

"Aijeleth Shahar" is part of the title of #Ps 22:1 and probably describes to the musician the melody to which the psalm was to be played [‘ayeleth is doe or deer in Hebrew and Shahar is morning or dawn]

to destroy us? I know thee who thou art, the Holy One of God.

“The same Psalm says: ‘All they that see me laugh me to scorn: they shoot out the lip, they shake the head, saying, He trusted on the Lord that he would deliver him: let him deliver him, seeing he delighted in him.’ (Ps. 27:7–8.) The fulfillment, as Jesus hung on the cross, is found in these words: ‘The chief priests mocking him, with the scribes and elders, said, he saved others; himself he…

plea to his Father, Jesus was also referencing the 22nd Psalm by invoking the first line of the chapter. Had those present understood the reference, they could have opened their scriptures (so to speak) and read the Messianic psalm which graphically paints the scene of Christ’s crucifixion as foreseen by the prophet-king David. Consider this clear reference to Christ’s crucifixion: ‘My God, my…

"He calls to God and He answers him!": possibly an allusion to the taunt at 5:1; cf. the famous taunt in Psalms 22:9, "He trusted on the Lord that He would deliver him: let Him deliver him, seeing He delighted in him" (quoted from the AV). (Raymond P. Scheindlin, The Book of Job, W. W. Norton & Company New York, 1998, pp 175)

PSALM XXII, David prayeth in great distress, yet he praiseth God, To the chief Musician upon Aijeleth Shahar, A Psalm of David. Y God, why hast. thou forsaken’ me? My God, hear the words of ‘my roaring; thou art far from helping me.
COMPASSED. Nasabbu (compassed) is a nifal.

Jesus said, 'I thirst,' but the Psalms give a greater description of what the Master was feeling: 'I am poured out like water, and all my bones are out of joint: my heart is like wax; it is melted in the midst of my bowels. My strength is dried up like a potsherd; and my tongue cleaveth to my jaws; and thou hast brought me into the dust of death.' (Psalms 22:14-15)

"Thou hast brought me into the dust of death," the Psalmist continues, "For dogs have compassed me, the assembly of the wicked have inclosed me: they pierced my hands and my feet," which is exactly what transpired on the gloomy day of crucifixion. Then this: "They part my garments among them, and cast lots upon my vesture" (Ps. 22:15-18), of which prediction Matthew says, "And they crucified him,…

Took his garments, and made four parts… cast lots This is a fulfillment of Psalm 22: 18: "they part my garments among them, and cast lots upon by vesture." It was custom among the Jews to wear five articles of clothing: a head-dress, shoes, an inner garment, and outer garment, and a girdle. Upon crucifixion these articles naturally fell to the ownership of the Roman soldiers. Dividing the…

church [Gr. ekklesia] "As found in our Bible, the Messianic prophecy here quoted, reads: 'I will declare thy name unto my brethren: in the midst of the congregation will I praise thee.' (Psalms 22:22) Interestingly, the Old Testament expression, 'congregation,' is quoted by Paul as, 'church,' thus indicating that he knew, as the Book of Mormon also testifies, that there was an organized Church in…
